input.initialPrompt: We changed the mapping of the editor colours to align with the new foreground palette. Can you fiugure out the mapping between the old slot numbers and the new. We need to update the theme files.

data.content: I’m going to trace the editor palette definitions in code and docs, then derive an old→new slot mapping you can apply to theme files.
data.reasoningText:
**Analyzing slot mapping**

I need to determine how to map old slot numbers to new ones based on the code or references. It looks like this won’t be a quick fix, so I should probably inspect the repo and create a plan.md since the user's request likely involves more investigation than a simple code change. I should also consider reporting intent with other tools and exploring how an agent could help synthesize this mapping efficiently. Let's focus on maximizing parallel efforts!**Creating a plan**

I need to create a plan.md, possibly using apply_patch sequentially after doing some initial reads. It seems like sending a preamble first could set the right stage for what I'm trying to accomplish. I want to ensure everything flows smoothly and makes sense for the user. My goal is to keep the process organized while also being thorough, so that I can clearly address the task at hand. Let's make sure to get this right!

./reference/Pico_Logo_Reference.md-522-`command`
./reference/Pico_Logo_Reference.md-523-
./reference/Pico_Logo_Reference.md:524:Starts the Pico Logo Editor with all procedures and variables. Procedures are formatted using [`to`](#to)/[`end`](#end) syntax, variables as [`make`](#make) commands, and property lists as [`pprop`](#pprop) commands. This is the same output as [`poall`](#poall). The format ensures that when you exit the editor, all definitions can be re-executed to recreate the workspace state.

./reference/Pico_Logo_Reference.md:535:Stands for `ed`it `n`ame (name must be quoted). Starts the Logo Editor with the variable named _name_ (or variables in the list _namelist_) and their values in it. This is the same output as [`pon`](#pon). When you exit the editor the [`make`](#make) are run, so whatever variables and values have been changed in the editor are changed in Logo.

./reference/Pico_Logo_Reference.md-4047-# Appendix E: Colour Palette for Pico Logo
./reference/Pico_Logo_Reference.md-4048-
./reference/Pico_Logo_Reference.md:4049:This is the colour palette for Pico Logo. The palette contains 256 colour numbers. The standard palette includes a range of colours across hues and includes greyscale colours.
./reference/Pico_Logo_Reference.md-4050-
./reference/Pico_Logo_Reference.md-4051-The first 16 colour numbers are the used by the text screen. Each character on the text screen has a foreground colour and a background colour, each of which is a colour number. The colour used for the foreground and background can be changed using [`settextcolor`](#textcolor-tc). The default foreground colour number is 3 (white) and the default background colour number is 4 (dark background).
./reference/Pico_Logo_Reference.md-4052-
./reference/Pico_Logo_Reference.md:4053:These colour numbers are assigned to the following purposes. You can change the colour stored in these colour numbers to customise the the text screen and editor's appearance:
./reference/Pico_Logo_Reference.md-4054-
./reference/Pico_Logo_Reference.md-4055-| Colour Number | Purpose |
--
./reference/Pico_Logo_Reference.md-4072-| 15 | Bracket depth 3 |
./reference/Pico_Logo_Reference.md-4073-
./reference/Pico_Logo_Reference.md:4074:The following 160 colour numbers are the default palette for Pico Logo, and changing these colour numbers should be avoided.
./reference/Pico_Logo_Reference.md-4075-
./reference/Pico_Logo_Reference.md:4076:Colour numbers 176 through 247 are unallocated and can be used for your own purposes. You can change the colour stored in these colour numbers to create your own palette.
./reference/Pico_Logo_Reference.md-4077-
./reference/Pico_Logo_Reference.md-4078-Colour numbers 248 through 253 hold the primary and secondary colours. Changing this range of colour numbers should be avoided.

./reference/Pico_Logo_Reference.md-415-
./reference/Pico_Logo_Reference.md:416:The Logo Editor is an interactive screen-oriented text editor, which provides a flexible way to define and change Logo instructions. The main command for starting up the Logo Editor is [`edit`](#edit-ed).

./reference/Pico_Logo_Reference.md-492-If the instructions in the edit buffer define a procedure (that is, if there is a title line `to` ... that starts the definition), Logo behaves as though you had typed the definition of the procedure using `to`. If the buffer contains a procedure definition, but there is no `end` instruction at the end of the buffer, Logo helps out by ending the definition for you.
./reference/Pico_Logo_Reference.md-493-
./reference/Pico_Logo_Reference.md:494:If there are Logo instructions on lines in the edit buffer that are part of the definition of a procedure, Logo caries them out when you exit the editor. Logo will not carry out any graphics commands or editing commands.
./reference/Pico_Logo_Reference.md-495-
./reference/Pico_Logo_Reference.md-496-In the Editor, you may define more than one procedure at a
./reference/Pico_Logo_Reference.md-497-time as long as each procedure is terminated by `end`.
./reference/Pico_Logo_Reference.md-498-
./reference/Pico_Logo_Reference.md:499:Exiting the editor using `Brk`, Logo does not read any lines in the edit buffer. If you were defining a procedure, the definition will be the same before you started editing.

./reference/Pico_Logo_Reference.md-523-
./reference/Pico_Logo_Reference.md:524:Starts the Pico Logo Editor with all procedures and variables. Procedures are formatted using [`to`](#to)/[`end`](#end) syntax, variables as [`make`](#make) commands, and property lists as [`pprop`](#pprop) commands. This is the same output as [`poall`](#poall). The format ensures that when you exit the editor, all definitions can be re-executed to recreate the workspace state.

Starts the Logo Editor with the variable named _name_ (or variables in the list _namelist_) and their values in it. This is the same output as [`pon`](#pon). When you exit the editor the [`make`](#make) are run, so whatever variables and values have been changed in the editor are changed in Logo.

data.reasoningText:
**Investigating code revisions**

I need to inspect specific lines around the appendix, especially considering the current mapping. It might be useful to compare the old version, maybe before the eda160d commit. I should read the relevant file ranges and use git to show the previous commits. Additionally, creating a plan.md could help after I grasp the initial details. I'll focus on specific files and might use bash commands to help streamline this process. Let's get started!

arguments.command:
git --no-pager show eda160d^:reference/Pico_Logo_Reference.md | sed -n '4051,4080p' && printf '
---FILE-SPLIT---
' && git --no-pager show eda160d^:devices/picocalc/editor.c | sed -n '1,140p' && printf '
---FILE-SPLIT---
' && git --no-pager show eda160d^:devices/picocalc/lcd.c | sed -n '640,690p'

toolResult.textResultForLlm:
4051. The first 16 colour numbers are the used by the text screen. Each character on the text screen has a foreground colour and a background colour, each of which is a colour number. The colour used for the foreground and background can be changed using [`settextcolor`](#textcolor-tc). The default foreground colour number is 3 (white) and the default background colour number is 4 (dark background).
4052. 
4053. These colour numbers are assigned to the following purposes. You can change the colour stored in these colour numbers to customise the the text screen and editor's appearance:
4054. 
4055. | Colour Number | Purpose |
4056. |---|---|
4057. | 0 | Text foreground (0%) |
4058. | 1 | Text foreground (33%) |
4059. | 2 | Text foreground (66%) |
4060. | 3 | Text foreground (100%) |
4061. | 4 | Text background |
4062. | 5 | Error messages|
4063. | 6 | Strings (`"word`) |
4064. | 7 | Commands |
4065. | 8 | Procedure name (word after `to`) |
4066. | 9 | Numbers |
4067. | 10 | Comments |
4068. | 11 | Variables (`:name`) |
4069. | 12 | Keywords (`to`, `end`) |
4070. | 13 | Bracket depth 1 |
4071. | 14 | Bracket depth 2 |
4072. | 15 | Bracket depth 3 |
4073. 
4074. The following 160 colour numbers are the default palette for Pico Logo, and changing these colour numbers should be avoided.
4075. 
4076. Colour numbers 176 through 247 are unallocated and can be used for your own purposes. You can change the colour stored in these colour numbers to create your own palette.
4077. 
4078. Colour numbers 248 through 253 hold the primary and secondary colours. Changing this range of colour numbers should be avoided.
4079. 
4080. Colour number 254 is used as the default pen colour. Colour number 255 contains the current background colour used on the graphics screen and is set using [`setbg`](#setbg).

toolResult.textResultForLlm:
1. //
2. //  Pico Logo
3. //  Copyright 2026 Blair Leduc. See LICENSE for details.
4. //
5. //  PicoCalc full-screen text editor implementation
6. //
7. //  Based on the Pico Logo reference manual editor specification:
8. //  - Header: "PICO LOGO EDITOR" (centered, reverse video)
9. //  - Footer: "ESC - ACCEPT    BRK - CANCEL" (centered, reverse video)
10. //  - 30 lines of editable text
11. //  - Cursor movement, insert/delete, block operations
12. //
13. 
14. #include "editor.h"
15. #include "keyboard.h"
16. #include "lcd.h"
17. #include "screen.h"
18. #include "devices/font.h"
19. #include "core/syntax_highlight.h"
20. 
21. #include <string.h>
22. #include <stdbool.h>
23. 
24. // Editor screen layout constants
25. #define EDITOR_HEADER_ROW     0      // Header at top of screen
26. #define EDITOR_FIRST_ROW      1      // First editable row
27. #define EDITOR_LAST_ROW       30     // Last editable row (30 lines)
28. #define EDITOR_FOOTER_ROW     31     // Footer at bottom
29. #define EDITOR_VISIBLE_ROWS   30     // Number of visible editable rows
30. #define EDITOR_MAX_COLS       40     // Maximum columns per line
31. #define EDITOR_SCROLL_MARGIN  5      // Columns from edge before horizontal scroll triggers
32. #define EDITOR_LEFT_ARROW     30     // Left arrow glyph (content scrolled left)
33. #define EDITOR_RIGHT_ARROW    31     // Right arrow glyph (content continues right)
34. 
35. // Tab width for indentation (2 spaces per tab stop)
36. #define TAB_WIDTH             2
37. 
38. // Syntax highlighting palette slots — use the text palette (slots 0-15)
39. #define PALETTE_SYNTAX_DEFAULT      3   // White
40. #define PALETTE_SYNTAX_COMMENT     10   // Comments (green)
41. #define PALETTE_SYNTAX_KEYWORD     12   // Keywords (purple)
42. #define PALETTE_SYNTAX_FUNCTION     8   // Procedures (yellow-green)
43. #define PALETTE_SYNTAX_VARIABLE    11   // Variables (cyan)
44. #define PALETTE_SYNTAX_STRING       6   // Strings (orange)
45. #define PALETTE_SYNTAX_NUMBER       9   // Numbers (green)
46. #define PALETTE_SYNTAX_COMMAND      7   // Commands (gold)
47. #define PALETTE_SYNTAX_BRACKET_1   13   // Bracket depth 1 (pink)
48. #define PALETTE_SYNTAX_BRACKET_2   14   // Bracket depth 2 (gold)
49. #define PALETTE_SYNTAX_BRACKET_3   15   // Bracket depth 3 (blue)
50. #define PALETTE_SYNTAX_BG           4   // Editor background
51. 
52. // Map SyntaxCategory enum values to palette slots
53. static const uint8_t category_to_palette[] = {
54.     [SYNTAX_DEFAULT]   = PALETTE_SYNTAX_DEFAULT,
55.     [SYNTAX_COMMENT]   = PALETTE_SYNTAX_COMMENT,
56.     [SYNTAX_KEYWORD]   = PALETTE_SYNTAX_KEYWORD,
57.     [SYNTAX_FUNCTION]  = PALETTE_SYNTAX_FUNCTION,
58.     [SYNTAX_VARIABLE]  = PALETTE_SYNTAX_VARIABLE,
59.     [SYNTAX_STRING]    = PALETTE_SYNTAX_STRING,
60.     [SYNTAX_NUMBER]    = PALETTE_SYNTAX_NUMBER,
61.     [SYNTAX_COMMAND]   = PALETTE_SYNTAX_COMMAND,
62.     [SYNTAX_BRACKET_1] = PALETTE_SYNTAX_BRACKET_1,
63.     [SYNTAX_BRACKET_2] = PALETTE_SYNTAX_BRACKET_2,
64.     [SYNTAX_BRACKET_3] = PALETTE_SYNTAX_BRACKET_3,
65. };
66. 
67. // Copy buffer size (default 1024 for RP2040, 8192 for RP2350)
68. #ifndef LOGO_COPY_BUFFER_SIZE
69. #define LOGO_COPY_BUFFER_SIZE 1024
70. #endif
71. 
72. // Dirty tracking flags
73. #define DIRTY_NONE      0x00    // No redraw needed
74. #define DIRTY_LINE      0x01    // Single line needs redraw
75. #define DIRTY_FROM_LINE 0x02    // Redraw from line to bottom
76. #define DIRTY_ALL       0x04    // Full screen redraw
77. #define DIRTY_CURSOR    0x08    // Just cursor position changed
78. 
79. // Editor state
80. typedef struct {
81.     char *buffer;           // Pointer to the edit buffer
82.     size_t buffer_size;     // Maximum buffer size
83.     size_t content_length;  // Current content length
84.     
85.     // Cursor position (in buffer coordinates)
86.     size_t cursor_pos;      // Cursor position in buffer (0-based)
87.     
88.     // View position (for scrolling)
89.     int view_start_line;    // First visible line index (vertical scroll)
90.     int h_scroll_offset;    // Horizontal scroll offset for current line
91.     
92.     // Selection state
93.     bool selecting;         // Whether selection is active
94.     size_t select_anchor;   // Start of selection (buffer position)
95.     
96.     // Copy buffer
97.     char copy_buffer[LOGO_COPY_BUFFER_SIZE];
98.     size_t copy_length;
99.     
100.     // Graphics preview state
101.     bool in_graphics_preview;  // True when viewing graphics screen (F3)
102.     
103.     // Dirty tracking for optimized redraws
104.     uint8_t dirty_flags;    // What needs to be redrawn
105.     int dirty_line;         // Line that needs redraw (for DIRTY_LINE)
106.     int dirty_from;         // First line to redraw (for DIRTY_FROM_LINE)
107. } EditorState;
108. 
109. static EditorState editor;
110. 
111. // Forward declarations
112. static void editor_draw_header(void);
113. static void editor_draw_footer(void);
114. static void editor_draw_content(void);
115. static void editor_draw_line(int screen_row, int line_index, int bracket_depth);
116. static void editor_position_cursor(void);
117. static void editor_insert_char(char c);
118. static void editor_insert_tab(void);
119. static void editor_delete_char(void);
120. static void editor_backspace(void);
121. static void editor_delete_selection(void);
122. static void editor_copy_selection(void);
123. static void editor_paste(void);
124. static void editor_move_cursor_left(void);
125. static void editor_move_cursor_right(void);
126. static void editor_move_cursor_up(void);
127. static void editor_move_cursor_down(void);
128. static void editor_move_cursor_home(void);
129. static void editor_move_cursor_end(void);
130. static void editor_page_up(void);
131. static void editor_page_down(void);
132. static void editor_new_line(void);
133. static int editor_get_line_start(int line_index);
134. static int editor_get_line_end(int line_index);
135. static int editor_get_line_at_pos(size_t pos);
136. static int editor_get_col_at_pos(size_t pos);
137. static int editor_count_lines(void);
138. static bool editor_ensure_cursor_visible(void);
139. static void editor_update_h_scroll(void);
140. static void editor_mark_line_dirty(int line_index);

data.content: I’ve confirmed the change: editor colours moved from the reserved high slots into the first 16 text/editor slots. I’m writing down the exact translation and checking whether any theme assets in-repo still use the old numbers.

toolRequests.name: apply_patch
toolRequests.arguments:
*** Begin Patch
*** Add File: /Users/blair/.copilot/session-state/a953c909-4cae-4525-9fa4-a92def00c9c6/plan.md
+Problem: editor syntax/theme palette slots were remapped from reserved high palette entries to the text/editor foreground palette, and we need an exact old-slot to new-slot translation to update theme files safely.
+
+Approach:
+- Read the current reference and editor/LCD palette definitions.
+- Read the pre-change definitions from git history.
+- Derive an exact old-slot -> new-slot mapping by semantic role.
+- Search the repository for theme files or palette assignments still using the old slot numbers.
+
+Todos:
+- inspect-palette-defs: confirm current and historical editor palette assignments.
+- derive-slot-mapping: produce the translation table for theme files and note any affected in-repo files.
+
+Notes:
+- Current reference appendix documents the new 0-15 text/editor palette roles.
+- Historical editor implementation used slots 240-247 for semantic colours, 250-252 for bracket depth, and 253 for editor background.
*** End Patch

data.reasoningText:
**Mapping editor color slots**

I’m considering the mapping from the old slots 240-247 and 250-253 to the new slots 0-15. I need to check if the changes align with the new foreground palette. The current document indicates the new mapping, but I'm unsure about the specifics of the old mapping and whether 240-253 were reserved for syntax highlighting.

The user asked about updating theme files based on this mapping. I think verifying the old numbers and confirming any documentation changes will be crucial before proceeding.**Clarifying theme updates**

I'm reflecting on the user's request about updating theme files; it seems they want me to focus on figuring out the mapping. To be cautious, I should identify which theme files need attention. They specifically asked for the mapping, so I’m considering that my response might be sufficient. 

I need to inspect the theme files, particularly those in assets or any references to `setpalette` with slots 240-253. It might also help to create an update plan since this could get a bit complex, noting my approach and to-dos.

 ./reference/Pico_Logo_Reference.md:1045:Your PicoCalc has 32 lines of text on the screen, with 40 characters on each line. You can use the screen entirely for text or entirely for graphics. The PicoCalc also lets you use the top 24 lines (240 turtle units) for graphics and the bottom eight for text at the same time. When you start up Logo, the entire screen is available for text.

data.content: I found all the shipped theme files, and they still use the old editor slots. I’m going to remap them in place now using the semantic-role translation from the code change.
data.reasoningText:
**Assessing theme files update**

I see that the theme files likely use old slots, and the user mentioned mapping. It seems like they might want some information on how to manage that. I guess it would make sense to proactively update the theme files while keeping track of the mapping between old and new slot numbers. I should figure out the file format to ensure a smooth update while being mindful that the user didn’t explicitly ask for editing.**Evaluating theme files updates**

I need to search through the theme files, which likely have similar patterns, involving mostly updates to slot numbers and comments. It seems I should consider editing these files and possibly the related documentation. I’m unsure if I need baseline tests since these are just data/theme files. However, the instructions suggest running linters, builds, and tests before and after changes. I could identify existing tests and run relevant ones, especially with `ctest`, to ensure everything remains functional.**Planning changes and tests**

It might take some time, but I want to ensure everything is in order before making changes. I should probably run some tests afterward, especially since updating data files typically won’t break C code, but theme parser changes might affect theme files. Conducting a simple syntax check for loaded theme files could be useful. I could run `ctest --test-dir build-tests --output-on-failure` for a baseline check. I also need to examine the theme files' contents and consider using a script to update identical slot comments.

data.content: The remap is direct by semantic role, not numeric order. I’m rewriting the theme files so each existing colour value lands in its new semantic slot.

toolRequests.name: bash

arguments.command:
python3 - <<'PY'
from pathlib import Path
mapping = {
    '240': '3',
    '241': '10',
    '242': '12',
    '243': '8',
    '244': '11',
    '245': '6',
    '246': '9',
    '247': '7',
    '250': '13',
    '251': '14',
    '252': '15',
    '253': '4',
}
replacements = {
    '; [Semantic colours - slots 240-247]': '; [Editor semantic colours in text/editor palette slots]',
    '; [Bracket pair colours (slots 250-252)]': '; [Bracket pair colours (slots 13-15)]',
    '; [Editor background (slot 253)]': '; [Editor background (slot 4)]',
}
for path in sorted(Path('logo/themes').glob('*.theme')):
    text = path.read_text()
    for old, new in replacements.items():
        text = text.replace(old, new)
    lines = []
    for line in text.splitlines():
        stripped = line.strip()
        if stripped.startswith('setpalette '):
            parts = stripped.split(None, 2)
            if len(parts) >= 3 and parts[1] in mapping:
                prefix = line[: line.index('s')]
                line = f"{prefix}setpalette {mapping[parts[1]]} {parts[2]}"
        lines.append(line)
    path.write_text('\n'.join(lines) + '\n')
PY

git --no-pager diff -- logo/themes

data.reasoningText:
**Updating theme files**

I need to edit some theme files. I’m considering using a script in Perl or Ruby since I might not have Python installed. The mapping is straightforward, so I’ll inspect specific files but not all of them. I want to update comments regarding semantic colors—specifically slots 0-15—and ensure clarity about the text and editor foreground palette slots. I might say something like, "Text foreground and editor colors use slots 0-15; this theme sets the editor-related ones below." I’ll keep it minimal.**Modifying theme files**

I need to modify nine theme files and consider using a script for this task. Before starting, I should check the `default.theme` file for comments and update the first lines for consistency. I’ll verify if `python3` is available, but I might end up using Perl with Bash as well. I plan to transform specific numbers and comments while creating backups. I should also consider updating related documentation, like the README, and search for old slots through theme files for verification. After finishing, I’ll run tests to ensure everything works properly.

toolArgs.command:
python3 - <<'PY'
from pathlib import Path
mapping = {
    '240': '3',
    '241': '10',
    '242': '12',
    '243': '8',
    '244': '11',
    '245': '6',
    '246': '9',
    '247': '7',
    '250': '13',
    '251': '14',
    '252': '15',
    '253': '4',
}
replacements = {
    '; [Semantic colours - slots 240-247]': '; [Editor semantic colours in text/editor palette slots]',
    '; [Bracket pair colours (slots 250-252)]': '; [Bracket pair colours (slots 13-15)]',
    '; [Editor background (slot 253)]': '; [Editor background (slot 4)]',
}
for path in sorted(Path('logo/themes').glob('*.theme')):
    text = path.read_text()
    for old, new in replacements.items():
        text = text.replace(old, new)
    lines = []
    for line in text.splitlines():
        stripped = line.strip()
        if stripped.startswith('setpalette '):
            parts = stripped.split(None, 2)
            if len(parts) >= 3 and parts[1] in mapping:
                prefix = line[: line.index('s')]
                line = f"{prefix}setpalette {mapping[parts[1]]} {parts[2]}"
        lines.append(line)
    path.write_text('\n'.join(lines) + '\n')
PY

git --no-pager diff -- logo/themes
toolArgs.description: Remap editor palette slots in theme files

Done — I traced the remap from the old reserved editor slots to the new text/editor palette slots and updated all shipped theme files in `logo/themes/*.theme`.

Old → new mapping:
`240→3`, `241→10`, `242→12`, `243→8`, `244→11`, `245→6`, `246→9`, `247→7`, `250→13`, `251→14`, `252→15`, `253→4`.

That matches the semantic roles in `devices/picocalc/editor.c` and the new table in `reference/Pico_Logo_Reference.md`.

I also verified no theme file still references the old slots, and `ctest --test-dir build-tests --output-on-failure` passed (`41/41`).
data.reasoningText:
**Clarifying updates**

I need to create a concise summary, ideally under 100 words. I should mention the mapping table updates that cover nine theme files and tests while also noting the source lines. It's important to highlight the mapping from old to new roles, like 240 to 3 for default and 241 to 10 for comments. I've updated all the logo/themes files, verified that no old slots remain, and passed the ctest. Including reference citations could be helpful, even if not strictly necessary.
